title: Pointers should not be cast to integral types
description: Pointers should not be cast to integral typesAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalreliabilityBugbased-on-misracertWhy is this an issue?More InfoThe size of integer required to hold a memory address is implementation-dependent. Therefore, casting a pointer (i.e. a memory address) to any integral data type may result in data loss because the integral type is too small to hold the full address value.When treating a memory address as integer type is absolutely required, you should be sure to use a large enough type to hold all the data.Noncompliant code exampleint *p; int addr = ( int ) &p;Available In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise CIDeveloper EditionAvailable Since9.1
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
int *p;
int addr = (int) &p; // Noncompliant: casting pointer to int may lose data

char *buffer;
short address = (short) buffer; // Noncompliant: casting pointer to short

void *ptr;
char c = (char) ptr; // Noncompliant: casting pointer to char
compliant:
int *p;
intptr_t addr = (intptr_t) &p; // Compliant: using intptr_t which can hold pointer

char *buffer;
uintptr_t address = (uintptr_t) buffer; // Compliant: uintptr_t is guaranteed to hold pointer

void *ptr;
size_t addr = (size_t) ptr; // Compliant: size_t typically large enough for pointers

checks.message: Pointers should not be cast to integral types. This can lead to loss of data and undefined behavior on different architectures.
